sections.title: What this blog is for
paragraphs: This blog shares practical notes on Shopify SEO, app quality, review trust, product updates, and the day-to-day work of building useful tools for merchants.
The focus is simple: useful context, honest opinions, and clear takeaways for store owners and app developers.

resourceCards.title: Let's Metrix for developers
resourceCards.description: An independent Shopify App Store analytics service that can help developers inspect app-review activity. It has no connection to autoBlogger.
items: Search for an app
Open the reviews view
Filter for deleted reviews
resourceCards.label: Open Let's Metrix

sections.title: A good idea has had a rough rollout
paragraphs: Shopify has started removing App Store reviews as part of a wider effort to tackle fake, incentivized, and otherwise untrusted feedback. I support that goal. Reviews influence which apps merchants install, so cleaning up manipulation is important for store owners and for developers who play by the rules.

The problem is that the rollout has made mistakes. Genuine reviews appear to have been caught alongside the reviews Shopify intended to remove. Shopify warned that this could happen when it announced the stronger detection system, and a [public follow-up](https://x.com/jzazove/status/2078284775459000349?s=20) from Shopify's [Jonathan Zazove](https://x.com/jzazove) indicates that the team is looking into problems with the rollout.
That distinction matters: a removed review is not proof that an app developer did anything wrong. Sometimes the detection system has simply made the wrong call.

sections.title: autoBlogger has lost reviews from real, paying customers
paragraphs: autoBlogger has been affected too. Reviews have disappeared from merchants who have used and paid for the app for more than two years. These are not anonymous accounts with no meaningful relationship to the product. They are long-standing customers with real subscription histories and genuine support interactions.

Losing feedback like that is frustrating because each honest review represents trust earned over time. It also feels unfair when legitimate developers lose hard-won reviews while the broader problem of manipulated review activity is still being addressed.
Shopify is investigating, and I hope the affected reviews are assessed using the underlying customer history rather than only the signal that triggered an automated removal.

sections.title: How to find reviews that may have been removed
paragraphs: Shopify does not provide developers with every detail behind its detection systems. An independent tool can still help you build an initial list of reviews that have disappeared.

Go to the [Let's Metrix developer page](https://letsmetrix.com/for-developers), search for your app, open the Reviews view, and switch the filter to Deleted. Let's Metrix has no connection to me or autoBlogger; it is simply a useful third-party place to start.

Treat that list as a lead rather than definitive proof. Compare it with your own support, billing, install, and customer records before contacting Shopify. Also check whether each review was unpublished or archived: [Shopify's review policy](https://help.shopify.com/partners/faq/reviews) allows developers to challenge a mistaken unpublishing, but says archived reviews cannot be appealed.
items: Open Let's Metrix for developers and find your app.
Select Reviews and change the filter to Deleted.
Record the merchant, review date, rating, and review text where available.
Match each result with your own customer records.
Confirm whether Shopify unpublished or archived the review before trying to appeal.

sections.title: Send Shopify evidence, not just a review count
paragraphs: If you believe a review was removed incorrectly, contact Shopify through the official route. [Shopify's help page](https://help.shopify.com/partners/faq/reviews) says to reply directly to the removal notification email and include a detailed explanation plus supporting evidence. If you need help locating the case, contact Shopify Partner Support.

For each affected store, explain the genuine relationship clearly. Include dated customer-support conversations, app install history, subscription or billing records, and the length of time the merchant used the app. In autoBlogger's case, the strongest evidence is straightforward: some affected reviewers have used and paid for the app for over two years.
Only send information that is relevant, protect merchant privacy, and use an official Shopify channel. A tidy table with one row per store will be easier to assess than a long emotional email.
items: Store domain or Shopify identifier
The removed review and its original date
Install and subscription dates
Invoices or billing records showing a genuine paid relationship
Dated support tickets or conversations showing real product use
A short explanation of why the review complies with Shopify's policy

sections.title: A simple appeal email developers can adapt
paragraphs: Keep the message factual and make it easy for the reviewer at Shopify to verify each case. For example: Subject: Evidence for genuine reviews removed from [App name]. Hello Shopify Partner Support, I believe the attached reviews were unpublished in error. For each store, I have included the review date, subscription history, billing evidence, and dated support interactions showing genuine use of our app. Please ask Partner Governance to reassess these reviews. I am happy to provide any further evidence you need. Thank you.

Attach or link to a concise evidence table. Do not include sensitive merchant data that Shopify does not need, and do not contact merchants asking them to recreate a review while the original decision is being investigated.

sections.title: I hope these are teething issues—not a reason to stop
paragraphs: I hope the current mistakes are teething issues in a system that will improve quickly. Shopify needs a way to correct false positives, communicate clearly with developers, and restore genuine reviews when the evidence supports them.

Once that process is working properly, I also hope we begin to see meaningful action against developers who have deliberately manipulated reviews. Removing questionable reviews is useful, but lasting trust will require consistent enforcement against the behaviour behind them.

I am happy for autoBlogger to take a few hits if the final result is a better Shopify App Store for everyone. Genuine reviews should be restored, bad actors should face real consequences, and merchants should be able to trust that the feedback they see reflects real experience. That is still an outcome worth pursuing.

faq.question: How can I find reviews removed from my Shopify app listing?
faq.answer: One option is the independent Let's Metrix developer tool. Search for your app, open its reviews, and change the filter to Deleted. Confirm the results against your own records because it is a third-party service and is not connected with autoBlogger or Shopify.

faq.question: Can a Shopify app review removal be appealed?
faq.answer: Shopify says developers who believe a review was unpublished by mistake can reply to the removal notification email with a detailed explanation and evidence. Shopify currently says archived review decisions cannot be appealed, so first confirm whether a review was unpublished or archived.

faq.question: What evidence should I send Shopify?
faq.answer: Useful evidence can include dated customer-support conversations, app subscription and billing history, install history, and other records showing that the store owner genuinely used and paid for the app. Protect merchant privacy and send the evidence only through an official Shopify support channel.

faq.question: Does losing a review mean the app developer did something wrong?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. Shopify has said that genuine apps may also see reviews unpublished when those reviews are connected to activity its systems consider untrustworthy.

sections.title: What a Shopify Staff Pick means
paragraphs: A Shopify Staff Pick is an app Shopify chooses to feature in the App Store. It is not a badge developers can add themselves.
If you are comparing apps, it is one more reason to take a closer look.

sections.title: Why being featured twice stands out
paragraphs: autoBlogger appeared in the spotlight in 2024 and then again in 2026. Seeing both screenshots gives merchants a clearer proof point than a single unsourced claim.
It should still sit alongside reviews, pricing, features, and the Shopify App Store listing, but it is useful context when you are deciding what to install.

sections.title: How autoBlogger helps your store
paragraphs: autoBlogger is built for stores that want to keep publishing SEO-focused blog content without turning it into another job for the team.
It helps you publish posts that support products and collections, add internal links and metadata, include FAQ content where useful, and keep content moving without a heavy editorial process.

sections.title: Proof and update log
paragraphs: This page keeps the public Staff Pick proof in one place so merchants can check the screenshots before installing.

faq.question: When was autoBlogger featured as a Shopify Staff Pick?
faq.answer: The screenshots on this page show autoBlogger featured in the Shopify App Store spotlight in 2024 and 2026.

faq.question: Is a Shopify Staff Pick a paid badge?
faq.answer: No. It is an editorial placement chosen by Shopify, not something developers can pay for.

faq.question: Where can I learn more before installing?
faq.answer: The features page, reviews, pricing page, and Shopify App Store listing will give you the clearest picture before you install.

sections.title: The short answer: yes, autoblogging can work
paragraphs: Autoblogging works when it solves a real operational problem: turning useful store knowledge into clear articles on a dependable schedule. A good auto blogging workflow can help a small team cover customer questions, product use cases, comparisons, buying considerations, and seasonal topics that might otherwise remain unwritten.

What it cannot do is guarantee a position in Google, a fixed amount of traffic, or automatic sales. Search performance depends on the usefulness and accuracy of each article, the authority and technical health of the site, competition, search demand, and time. Autoblogging is a way to improve the publishing process; it is not a substitute for a sound product, a trustworthy store, or a sensible SEO strategy.
That distinction is encouraging rather than negative. It means AI auto blogging can create genuine value when it is used to help people, with realistic expectations and basic quality control.

sections.title: What autoblogging actually means
paragraphs: Autoblogging is the use of software to automate part or all of the blog production workflow. Depending on the tool, that can include choosing a relevant topic, drafting an article, formatting headings, preparing metadata, adding internal links, scheduling publication, and sharing the finished post.

Modern autoblogging AI is more capable than older systems that simply rearranged or copied existing text. It can produce a useful first version from a clear topic and store context. The strongest setup still treats the technology as a publishing assistant: give it relevant inputs, keep the scope connected to the business, and review important details.
For a Shopify merchant, the goal is not to publish the largest possible number of articles. It is to make consistent, useful publishing manageable.

sections.title: The practical benefits of AI auto blogging
paragraphs: The clearest benefit is time. Researching, outlining, drafting, formatting, linking, and publishing even one article can compete with product, fulfilment, support, and marketing work. Automation reduces that repeated workload and makes a realistic content schedule easier to maintain.

Consistency is another benefit. A useful blog grows article by article. Regular publishing gives a store more opportunities to answer specific questions and build a connected library around its products and expertise. It also avoids the familiar pattern of publishing several posts at launch and then leaving the blog untouched for months.

Auto blogging can also help a merchant cover more of the customer journey. Product pages usually need to stay concise, while blog posts can explain how to choose, compare, use, care for, or combine products in much more detail. Relevant internal links can then help readers continue to a related product, collection, or guide.
items: Less time spent on repetitive drafting, formatting, and publishing tasks
A consistent schedule that is easier for a small team to sustain
More coverage of genuine customer questions and long-tail topics
More useful paths from informational articles to products and collections
A repeatable workflow that can be reviewed and improved over time

sections.title: What Google says about autoblogging AI
paragraphs: Google's published guidance does not say that content is bad simply because AI helped create it. Google says its systems focus on the quality of content rather than how it was produced. Helpful, original, people-first content may perform well; low-value content does not gain an advantage just because it was generated quickly.

There is an important boundary. Google says using automation, including AI, mainly to manipulate search rankings is against its spam policies. Its current guidance also warns about producing many pages without adding value. That is why a focused article that answers a real customer question is a better use of autoblogging than a flood of thin posts across unrelated topics.

In practical terms, use AI to make good publishing more achievable. Check claims, remove generic filler, add real product knowledge where it helps, and make sure every article has a reason to exist for the store's audience.

sections.title: Why Shopify blog automation is a good fit for small teams
paragraphs: Shopify already gives online stores a built-in blogging engine, and Shopify's own Help Center describes blogging as a way to support SEO, build an audience, and generate traffic and sales. Automation helps merchants use that channel without needing a full-time writer or a complicated content operation.

A store already has useful source material: product details, care instructions, support questions, buying advice, customer use cases, and seasonal knowledge. Shopify blog automation can turn those inputs into a steady editorial plan and connect relevant articles back to the catalogue.

This can be particularly valuable in a focused niche. A specialist store may not need hundreds of broad articles. It may benefit far more from a smaller collection of accurate posts that answer the questions its actual customers ask.

sections.title: Where autoblogging falls short
paragraphs: A truthful assessment includes the limits. AI can misunderstand a product, invent a detail, use an unsuitable tone, or produce an article that sounds polished but says little. No responsible auto blogger should turn those risks into promises that every generated post is automatically ready to rank.

Automation also cannot create search demand. A well-written post about a topic nobody searches for may still be useful to existing customers, but it is unlikely to attract meaningful organic traffic. Likewise, a new store in a competitive market may need time, stronger product pages, external recognition, and technical improvements alongside blog content.

These limits are manageable. Keep topics close to the store's expertise, use accurate product inputs, review sensitive or technical claims, update posts when information changes, and assess performance in Search Console over a meaningful period.

sections.title: A better autoblogging workflow
paragraphs: Start with the customer, not a keyword list. Collect questions from support conversations, product reviews, on-site search, and Search Console. Group them into topics that genuinely match the products and the store's expertise. A keyword can help describe demand, but the article should satisfy the person behind the query.

Next, choose a sustainable publishing pace. One useful article a week is better than daily low-value content that nobody checks. Make sure each draft has a clear answer, accurate facts, a descriptive title, readable headings, and relevant links. Publish, then use impressions, clicks, click-through rate, and the queries for each page to learn what needs improving.

This is where autoBlogger is designed to help Shopify merchants: reducing the repeated work involved in producing SEO-focused posts, while supporting product links, metadata, FAQs, and a recurring publishing schedule. The merchant still brings the most important ingredients—the products, audience, expertise, and judgment.
items: Choose topics connected to real products and customer questions
Give the system accurate store and product context
Review facts, tone, and any claims that could affect a buying decision
Use descriptive metadata and relevant internal links
Publish at a pace the store can maintain
Measure page-level results and improve posts instead of expecting instant wins

sections.title: The honest verdict
paragraphs: Does autoblogging work? Yes—when success means making useful, consistent content achievable and giving a store more relevant ways to be discovered. It can save substantial publishing time, broaden topic coverage, and help connect customer questions with products.

The best results come from combining automation with relevance, accuracy, and oversight. Treat autoblogging AI as a capable workflow tool, keep expectations grounded, and judge it by the quality of the published library and its performance over time—not by promises of instant rankings.

faq.question: Does autoblogging work for SEO?
faq.answer: Autoblogging can support SEO by helping a store publish useful content consistently, but automation alone does not guarantee rankings, traffic, or sales. Topic relevance, accuracy, originality, site quality, competition, and time all affect results.

faq.question: Does Google penalize AI autoblogging?
faq.answer: Google says it focuses on content quality rather than whether AI helped produce it. Using automation primarily to manipulate rankings or publishing many low-value pages can violate its spam policies.

faq.question: What are the main benefits of auto blogging?
faq.answer: The main benefits are time savings, a more reliable publishing schedule, broader coverage of relevant customer questions, and an easier way to create content that links readers to useful products and collections.

faq.question: Is Shopify blog automation suitable for a small store?
faq.answer: It can be especially useful for a small store that has product knowledge but limited time for writing. The store should still review facts, brand voice, product details, and published posts.

faq.question: How long does autoblogging take to produce results?
faq.answer: There is no fixed timeline. Search engines need time to discover and assess pages, and results depend on the site, topic, competition, content quality, and demand. Measure progress over months rather than expecting immediate rankings.

/blog/shopify-blog-seo-guide.resourceSectionIntro: These recommendations are grounded in current guidance from Shopify and Google Search Central.

resourceCards.title: SEO overview for Shopify stores
resourceCards.description: Shopify's overview covers relevant keywords, content optimization, site structure, internal linking, and Search Console.
resourceCards.label: Read Shopify's SEO overview

resourceCards.title: Blogs for your Shopify online store
resourceCards.description: Shopify explains how its built-in blogging engine can support SEO, audience building, traffic, and customer relationships.
resourceCards.label: Read Shopify's blog guide

resourceCards.title: Creating helpful, reliable, people-first content
resourceCards.description: Google provides questions for assessing whether content offers original value and is made primarily for people.
resourceCards.label: Read Google's content guidance

sections.title: What Shopify blog SEO is meant to achieve
paragraphs: Shopify blog SEO is the process of publishing articles that answer relevant searches and connect those readers with useful parts of an online store. The aim is not simply to add more URLs. It is to create pages that help potential customers research a problem, compare options, use a product, or make a better buying decision.

A blog gives a merchant room to answer questions that do not fit naturally on a product or collection page. A specialist cookware shop can explain how to choose a pan material. A skincare store can publish a routine for a particular skin concern. A cycling shop can help riders prepare for winter conditions. Each article can serve a search need while demonstrating real product knowledge.
That relevance is the foundation. Search-friendly formatting can make a strong article easier to understand, but it cannot make an unrelated or unhelpful topic valuable to the store's audience.

sections.title: Start with customer intent, not keyword repetition
paragraphs: Begin with the questions behind a search. Someone searching for how to wash a wool blanket needs instructions. Someone searching for the best blanket for a cold bedroom is comparing choices. Those two people need different articles even if both searches mention blankets.

Useful topic sources already exist inside most businesses: support tickets, live chat, product reviews, returns, on-site search, social comments, sales conversations, and Google Search Console. Look for repeated wording, uncertainty, and objections. Then choose subjects where the store can add accurate advice rather than restating generic information.

Use a clear primary phrase to keep the article focused, but write naturally around the whole question. Related terms and examples will appear as the topic is explained properly. Repeating an exact keyword in every paragraph makes an article harder to read and does not add expertise.
items: Identify the reader and the decision they are trying to make
Check that the topic connects naturally to the store's products or expertise
Choose one clear purpose for the article
Cover the follow-up questions a reader is likely to have

sections.title: Optimize the title, structure, and search listing
paragraphs: Give each post a descriptive title that tells a reader what they will learn. Put the main subject near the beginning when that reads naturally, and avoid vague headlines that depend on curiosity alone. The page should then use one clear main heading and descriptive subheadings that divide the answer into logical sections.

Write a short URL handle that reflects the topic and remains useful if the article is updated later. In Shopify's search engine listing settings, add a distinct page title and meta description. The description should summarize the benefit of the post and give a searcher a reason to choose it; it is a preview, not a place to list every keyword.

Make the body easy to scan with short paragraphs, lists where they genuinely clarify steps or options, and descriptive image alt text. Before publishing, check the article on mobile as well as desktop and remove introductions that delay the answer.
items: A specific, descriptive article title
A readable hierarchy of H2 and H3 subheadings
A concise URL handle
A unique SEO title and meta description
Accurate alt text for useful images

sections.title: Connect blog posts to products and related guides
paragraphs: Internal links turn an isolated article into part of the store. Link to a product or collection when it helps the reader act on the advice, and link to related articles when another guide answers the natural next question. Use anchor text that describes the page rather than generic wording such as click here.

The relationship should work in both directions. A strong evergreen guide can link to a collection, while a relevant collection description or another established article can link back to the guide. This gives shoppers more ways to discover it and helps search engines understand how the pages relate.

Avoid inserting every product into every post. A few contextual links are more useful than a block of unrelated destinations. Revisit links when products are discontinued, collection handles change, or a better supporting article is published.

sections.title: Publish consistently without sacrificing quality
paragraphs: There is no universal publishing frequency that guarantees SEO results. Choose a pace that leaves enough time for relevant topics, accurate details, useful structure, and a final review. For one store that may be weekly; for another, two strong posts each month may be more realistic.

A repeatable workflow makes consistency easier. Keep a topic backlog, record the customer question and intended destination for each post, draft in batches where appropriate, and schedule publication. Automation can reduce the work involved in outlining, drafting, formatting, linking, and publishing, but the store remains responsible for what appears on its site.

Review product specifications, health or safety statements, prices, availability, and any claim that could affect a purchase. Add examples from the business wherever they make the answer more useful and distinctive.

sections.title: Measure results and improve the content library
paragraphs: SEO content should be evaluated over time rather than judged immediately after publication. Use Google Search Console to see whether a post is earning impressions, which queries show it, its average position, and whether searchers click. Use store analytics to check whether readers continue to products, join a mailing list, or complete another useful action.

Different patterns suggest different updates. Impressions without clicks may point to an unclear title or description. Rankings for unexpected queries may reveal that the answer needs a clearer focus. Traffic without meaningful engagement may mean the article does not provide an obvious next step. A good post can also become outdated as products, advice, or customer expectations change.
The practical goal is a connected library that becomes more useful with each update. Publish deliberately, learn from real queries and customer behaviour, and improve the pages that show potential.
items: Search impressions and query relevance
Organic clicks and click-through rate
Visits from articles to products or collections
Conversions or assisted conversions
Accuracy and freshness of older posts

sections.title: How to choose an ecommerce blog idea
paragraphs: A useful Shopify blog idea has three parts: a real audience question, a clear connection to the store, and enough substance for a genuinely helpful answer. Topics built this way can attract relevant visitors without making the blog feel like a list of forced product promotions.

Before drafting, write down who the article is for, what they need to decide or do, and which page would be a natural next step. Check support conversations, on-site search, product reviews, social comments, seasonal buying patterns, and Search Console for language customers already use.

The 25 ideas below are templates. Replace the brackets with a specific product, audience, use case, problem, or season from your own niche. The narrower and more accurate the answer, the more useful it is likely to be.

sections.title: Buying guides that support product discovery
paragraphs: Buying guides work well before a customer has chosen a product. They should explain meaningful differences and help the reader narrow the options rather than simply declaring every item the best.
items: How to choose the right [product] for [specific use case]
A beginner's guide to buying [product category]
What size [product] do you need? A practical sizing guide
[Material A] vs [Material B]: which is right for you?
The best [product type] for [audience, budget, climate, or goal]

sections.title: How-to and product-use articles
paragraphs: How-to content serves customers who want to get a result. Use clear steps, mention the tools or preparation required, show common mistakes, and link only to products that genuinely help complete the task.
items: How to use [product] for the first time
How to get [desired result] with [product or method]
Seven ways to use [versatile product]
How to set up [product] in under [realistic time]
Common [activity] mistakes and how to avoid them

sections.title: Care, troubleshooting, and after-purchase content
paragraphs: Post-purchase questions are often overlooked, but helpful answers can build confidence before a sale as well as support existing customers. Keep instructions aligned with the actual product and manufacturer guidance.
items: How to clean and care for [product or material]
How long should [product] last?
Why is my [product] doing [common problem]?
When should you repair or replace [product]?
How to store [product] between seasons

sections.title: Inspiration, comparisons, and customer stories
paragraphs: Inspirational posts help shoppers picture a product in their own life. Use original examples, real customer permission where needed, and balanced comparisons that acknowledge which option suits which situation.
items: Ten [room, outfit, recipe, or project] ideas using [product]
Before and after: how [approach or product] changed [result]
A customer's guide to solving [specific problem]
[Product A] vs [Product B]: key differences explained
Meet the maker: how [product] is designed or produced

sections.title: Seasonal and occasion-led articles
paragraphs: Seasonal content should be prepared before demand peaks so there is time for discovery and promotion. Refresh durable guides each year instead of creating near-duplicate versions with only the date changed.
items: A [holiday or occasion] gift guide for [specific audience]
How to prepare your [home, wardrobe, hobby, or routine] for [season]
The [season] checklist for [customer goal]
What to pack for [event or trip]
Last-minute [occasion] ideas that can still feel thoughtful

sections.title: Turn the list into a focused content plan
paragraphs: Do not publish all 25 ideas simply because they are available. Score each candidate by customer relevance, product fit, available expertise, seasonal timing, and whether the store already has a page answering the same question. Choose the strongest cluster and build depth around it.

For example, a specialist tea store might connect a beginner's buying guide to articles on water temperature, steeping time, teapot materials, storage, and gift selection. The posts answer different questions while reinforcing the same area of expertise and creating natural internal links.

Keep a simple calendar with the target reader, main question, related products or collections, publication date, and update date. This turns scattered ideas into a useful, maintainable content library rather than a queue of disconnected posts.
items: Prioritize topics closest to customer questions and commercial relevance
Avoid producing several pages that answer the same intent
Plan internal links before drafting
Schedule seasonal pieces early
Review performance and expand the clusters that earn relevant interest

/blog/shopify-internal-linking-guide.resourceSectionIntro: The approach below follows official Shopify and Google guidance on site structure, crawlable links, and descriptive anchor text.

resourceCards.title: Optimizing Shopify site structure for search engines
resourceCards.description: Shopify recommends logical menus, accessible pages, and descriptive text for internal links.
resourceCards.label: Read Shopify's site structure guide

resourceCards.title: SEO link best practices
resourceCards.description: Google explains how crawlable links and concise, relevant anchor text help people and search engines understand pages.
resourceCards.label: Read Google's link guidance

resourceCards.title: SEO overview for Shopify stores
resourceCards.description: Shopify's overview describes internal links as part of a logical information hierarchy connecting related pages.
resourceCards.label: Read Shopify's SEO overview

sections.title: What internal linking does for a Shopify store
paragraphs: An internal link connects one page on a store to another page on the same domain. Navigation menus and breadcrumbs are internal links, but merchants can also add contextual links inside blog posts, collection descriptions, product copy, FAQs, and other pages.

For shoppers, a useful link answers what next. A care guide can point to the product it explains. A buying guide can lead to the appropriate collection. A product page can link to detailed sizing or installation advice. These paths reduce the work required to find relevant information.

For search engines, links provide discovery paths and context about relationships between pages. A logical network of links makes it easier to see that several guides, products, and collections belong to the same topic. Internal linking is not a shortcut to rankings, but it is a practical part of a clear site structure.

sections.title: Build simple product and topic clusters
paragraphs: Start with the store's main collections and the customer questions attached to them. Treat a collection or comprehensive guide as the centre of a small topic cluster, then connect supporting articles that cover selection, use, care, comparison, and troubleshooting.

A running store might have a trail shoes collection supported by guides about choosing grip, measuring fit, cleaning muddy shoes, and deciding when to replace them. Each guide can link to the collection and to another article where it is genuinely helpful. The collection can also link back to the strongest evergreen guidance.
Keep the map understandable. If a link exists only because two pages share a keyword, it may not help a reader. Link pages because the destination adds information or offers a sensible next action.
items: Choose one important collection, product group, or customer problem
List the distinct questions people ask around it
Match each question to an existing or planned page
Connect the pages where a reader would benefit from continuing

sections.title: Use anchor text that describes the destination
paragraphs: Anchor text is the clickable wording of a link. It should give readers a reasonable expectation of the page they will open. Descriptive phrases such as merino wool care guide or waterproof hiking jackets are more useful than click here, learn more, or a pasted web address.

Keep the wording natural in the sentence and concise enough to scan. It does not need to repeat the destination page's exact title, and every link to the same page does not need identical wording. Avoid turning whole paragraphs into links or packing several keyword-heavy links into one sentence.
The surrounding context matters too. A descriptive link in a relevant paragraph is clearer than the same link dropped into an unrelated footer list.

sections.title: Add links in both useful directions
paragraphs: New blog posts often link to products, but older pages should also help readers discover the new article. After publishing, identify two or three relevant established posts, collections, or pages and add a contextual link where the new guide improves the journey.

This habit prevents useful content from becoming orphaned, meaning it has no meaningful internal links pointing to it. A page can appear in a sitemap and still be difficult for customers to encounter through normal browsing. Linking from an established relevant page gives it a real place in the site structure.

Also connect related articles laterally. A guide to choosing a coffee grinder can naturally lead to an article about grind size. The second article can link back when choosing equipment is a useful prerequisite.

sections.title: Avoid common internal linking mistakes
paragraphs: More links are not automatically better. Repeated site-wide links, unrelated product blocks, and long lists of destinations can distract shoppers without adding context. Give priority to the pages that best answer the next question.

Check that links use normal crawlable HTML and point directly to the intended canonical URL. Avoid chains of redirects where the destination has moved more than once. If a product is permanently removed, update the surrounding sentence as well as the URL so the recommendation remains accurate.

Do not create multiple near-identical articles and rely on links to separate them. If two pages satisfy the same search need, consolidate or clarify their roles. A clean content structure is easier to link and easier for readers to understand.
items: Vague anchor text such as click here
Links to unrelated products added only for promotion
Broken links and unnecessary redirect chains
New posts with no links from established pages
Several articles competing to answer the same question

sections.title: Run a lightweight internal link review
paragraphs: Review internal links whenever a product range changes and as part of a quarterly content check. Begin with priority collections and the blog posts receiving organic impressions. Confirm that every important article has at least one relevant route from another page and offers a sensible next step of its own.

Look for broken destinations, outdated product recommendations, generic anchor text, orphaned posts, and pages with so many links that the main path is unclear. Search Console and analytics can show which articles attract visitors and where those readers continue, while a crawler can help inventory links at larger scale.

Finish by adding a few high-value links, not hundreds of mechanical ones. A maintained structure that reflects the current catalogue and customer journey is more useful than a one-time linking project.
items: Check priority pages for relevant incoming links
Repair broken or redirected destinations
Update links affected by catalogue changes
Replace vague anchors with descriptive wording
Add links between new articles and established related pages

resourceCards.title: Shopify is stepping up on fake reviews

items: Merchant trust
Review enforcement
App Store quality
resourceCards.label: Read the follow-up

sections.title: Why I am writing about Shopify fake reviews
paragraphs: I am Ollie Hermans, owner and developer of autoBlogger, autoLLMs, autoSchema, autoLock, autoShip, autoStockist, and autoBuy. For years I have been raising concerns about fake Shopify App Store reviews, incentivized reviews, and the way manipulated review signals can affect which apps merchants trust.

This has never been about knocking genuine competition. It has been about fairness. Merchants should be able to compare Shopify apps using real feedback, and developers who build carefully should not be competing against inflated review activity.
That is why Shopify's announcement matters. It is a clear step in the direction many people in the developer community have been asking for.

sections.title: What Shopify has announced
paragraphs: Shopify has announced two updates aimed at strengthening trust in App Store reviews. The first is a dedicated App Store requirement around review incentivization. In plain English, apps should not offer, unlock, or withhold features in exchange for a merchant leaving a review.

The second is stronger fake-review detection. Shopify says it is expanding the signals used to decide whether a review is authentic, and those signals will apply to both existing reviews and new reviews.
items: Apps found incentivizing reviews can lose a significant portion of reviews.
Reviews that do not meet Shopify's authenticity bar may be unpublished.
The rollout applies to old and new Shopify App Store reviews.
Some genuine apps may lose reviews if those reviews are connected to untrustworthy reviewer activity.

sections.title: Why this feels like a useful step forward
paragraphs: For a few years now, I have been sharing examples, starting conversations, and encouraging other developers to speak up about review quality on the Shopify App Store. Plenty of developers who build honestly have been frustrated by the same issue.

Fake reviews and incentivized reviews create an uneven playing field. They can make weaker apps look stronger than they are, make honest developers work uphill, and make merchants spend more time trying to judge which reviews are real.
So yes, I am genuinely pleased to see Shopify talk publicly about stronger enforcement. It is the kind of progress many of us have been hoping to see.

sections.title: What honest review collection should look like
paragraphs: The right way to ask for Shopify app reviews is simple: ask real users at the right moment, use neutral language, and never offer anything in return. A review should be feedback, not a transaction.

Negative reviews should not be buried, bought away, or drowned out. They should be answered properly. Merchants notice when developers engage with feedback in a useful way, and that kind of trust is worth more than a polished review count.
items: Ask after a merchant has actually used the app.
Keep the review request neutral and optional.
Do not unlock features, discounts, credits, or support in exchange for a review.
Reply to criticism with context, fixes, or a real support path.

sections.title: What I hope happens next
paragraphs: I hope this leads to a Shopify App Store where stronger products, better support, and genuine merchant feedback carry more weight than questionable review patterns.

The rollout will be worth watching closely. Review counts may change, some removals may be noisy, and Shopify will need to keep refining the signals. But if the result is a review system merchants can trust more, that is good for the whole ecosystem.
For my part, I will keep building the autoBlogger app family with the same focus: useful products, real support, honest feedback, and practical tools that help Shopify merchants grow.

faq.question: Why write about Shopify App Store reviews?
faq.answer: As the owner and developer of the autoBlogger app family, I have spent years raising concerns about fake and incentivized reviews on the Shopify App Store.

faq.question: What did Shopify announce about fake reviews?
faq.answer: Shopify announced a dedicated policy on review incentivization and stronger fake-review detection across both existing and new App Store reviews.

faq.question: Should developers ask merchants for reviews?
faq.answer: Yes, but requests should be neutral, optional, and aimed at merchants who have actually used the app.

faq.question: What should merchants do when comparing apps?
faq.answer: Look beyond the review count. Read recent reviews, check developer replies, review the app listing, and test whether the product fits your workflow.
